title-group.article-title: Clinical utility of the targeted next-generation sequencing for detection of Mycoplasma pneumoniae and antimicrobial resistance genes for adults

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/01me2d674grid.469593.40000 0004 1777 204XDepartment of Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine, Shenzhen Qianhai Shekou Free Trade Zone Hospital, Shenzhen, 518000 China
2https://ror.org/04f0j5d06Research Center for Healthy Aging and Social Economic Development, Guangzhou College of Commerce, Guangzhou, 510000 China

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Mycoplasma pneumoniae pneumonia (MPP) is common in children, and its incidence among adolescents and adults has been increasing in recent years. Targeted next-generation sequencing (tNGS) is a rapid, accurate and comprehensive method for pathogen detection; however, few studies have investigated its application value in the diagnosis of MPP.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Ninety patients (aged over 14 years ) were enrolled in this study and divided into MPP group (31 cases) and non-Mycoplasma pneumoniae pneumonia (non-MPP) group (59 cases). Clinical data, thin-slice spiral computed tomography (CT) scans of the lungs, tNGS results from bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) and blood samples, as well as other routine biochemical and traditional respiratory pathogen detection results, were collected. Clinical manifestations, etiological findings, and the performance of tNGS detection were compared between adolescent and adult patients with MPP and non-MPP for clinical reference.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Compared with the non-MPP group, adolescent and adult MPP patients exhibited distinct clinical features, including yellow sputum (35.5% vs. 88.1%, P = 0.03), headache (12.9% vs. 5.1%, P = 0.027), bronchial wall thickening (61.3% vs. 5.1%, P < 0.001), alveolar nodular exudative changes (51.6% vs. 3.9%, P < 0.001), and large airway sputum plugs (3.2% vs. 23.7%, P = 0.029). The sensitivity of tNGS in detecting Mycoplasma pneumoniae (M. pneumoniae) was 96.8%, which was superior to that of qPCR (80.6%) and IgM antibodies (67.7%). This technology can also simultaneously detect the coexistence of multiple pathogens with M. pneumoniae. Additionally, tNGS detected 10 antimicrobial resistance genes (ARGs) and 15 virulence factor genes in this study.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The imaging manifestations of MPP in adolescents and adults exhibit certain characteristics. Early recognition and timely diagnosis are the key to accurate diagnosis and improve prognosis. TNGS has superior diagnostic accuracy and provides essential data on resistance, its potential as a supplementary tool to assist in the quick detection of M. pneumoniae in adults remains promising.

sec.title: Clinical trial number
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12879-026-12770-4.

funding-source.institution: Science and Technology Innovation Bureau of Shenzhen Research Fund

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Mycoplasma pneumoniae (M. pneumoniae) is a common respiratory pathogen that mainly occurs in children and accounts for approximately 10% to 40% of pediatric community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) cases [1]. In recent years, the incidence of M. pneumoniae infections among adolescents and adults with CAP has shown an upward trend [2]. Notably, from 2023 to 2024, there has been an outbreak of MPP in China, mainly involving adolescents (14–18 years old) and adults (over 18 years old), including some severe MPP patients [3]. Delayed diagnosis not only increases the risk of infection in the surrounding population but also could cause serious pulmonary complications, such as chronic interstitial fibrosis, mechanical ventilation-associated pneumonia, and acute respiratory distress syndrome [4]. Therefore, timely and accurate diagnosis of MPP is essential for appropriate treatment, early rehabilitation, and reduction of complications [5]. Currently, the isolation of M. pneumoniae from the lesion site with a positive result is considered the standard for the etiological diagnosis of M. pneumoniae infection. However, M. pneumoniae typically requires two weeks of culture under specific conditions, and its sensitivity is less than 60%. Consequently, this method is generally used only for research purposes and has limited clinical utility. Although the IgM antibody of M. pneumoniae could indicate a recent infection, it generally appears 4–5 days after infection and peaks after 3–4 weeks. However, it is prone to false-negative results in the early stage of MPP, which may delay or mislead diagnosis and treatment [6, 7]. Although PCR is a rapid, simple and highly sensitive detection method, the persistence of M. pneumoniae after infection and asymptomatic carriage may cause false-positive results. False-negative results may also occur due to improper specimen processing, low DNA extraction efficiency, or mutations in the Mycoplasma gene [8]. The emergence of macrolide-resistant of Mycoplasma is becoming increasingly evident, and nucleic acid testing could not determine its drug resistance or virulence [9]. Therefore, there is an urgent clinical need for a rapid, accurate and comprehensive method to detect M. pneumoniae and its drug resistance.

As a new molecular diagnostic technology, targeted next-generation sequencing (tNGS) offers advantages such as detection sensitivity unaffected by human genome or background bacteria, lower costs, reduced sample transportation requirements, and the ability to quantitatively detect pathogens and antimicrobial resistance genes (ARGs) [10, 11]. tNGS has been increasingly used for pathogen detection in clinical infectious diseases. However, its value in detecting M. pneumoniae remains unclear. Currently, most studies on the clinical and pathogenic characteristics of MPP focus on children, with limited relevant information available for adolescents and adults. Our study aimed to evaluate the clinical utility of tNGS for M. pneumoniae detection. We also investigate the clinical characteristics of MPP and non-MPP in adolescent and adult patients.

sec.title: Populations and study design
sec.p: This prospective cohort study collected patients ( aged over 14 years ) with CAP admitted to the department of Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine at Shenzhen Qianhai Shekou Free Trade Zone Hospital between May 2023 to April 2024. Adolescents (14–18 years old) are similar to adults in terms of disease process, pharmacokinetics, and drug safety and tolerability, The 2019 Pediatric Innovation Research Forum advocated for the position that adolescents should routinely be considered for enrollment in phase 3 clinical trials [12]. Therefore, in current clinical practice, this age group is managed within the adult healthcare framework. The diagnosis of CAP was based on the 2007 guidelines issued by the American Thoracic Society [13]. The patient is required to complete a chest CT scan before admission or within 24 h of admission. All the computed tomography (CT) images of the patients are reviewed by our hospital’s radiology professor in a blinded manner. The assessment covers two aspects: the imaging manifestations of the affected lung lobe and pulmonary lesions. Patients were required to complete bronchoscopy within 24 h after admission. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) was collected from the most affected area of the lesion for tNGS testing, and blood samples were collected for tNGS detection. Clinical indicators, including blood routine, procalcitonin (PCT), electrolytes, C-reactive protein (CRP), myocardial enzymes, liver and kidney function and D-dimer, were recorded within 24 h of admission. Treatment strategies and relevant changes during follow-up were noted. Basic information, including gender, age, underlying diseases, and clinical symptoms was noted in their medical records.

Patients with the following complications were excluded: (1) immune dysfunction; (2) congenital heart disease; (3) altered consciousness or mental disorder The study was approved by the ethics committees of Shenzhen Qianhai Shekou Free Trade Zone Hospital (number: 2024KY-015–01 K).The participants provided written informed consent to participate in this study.

The diagnosis of MPP was independently determined by two respiratory experts selected in advance combined with the patient’s clinical characteristics, pulmonary imaging characteristics, and auxiliary examination results. According to the diagnosis, patients were diagnosed with MPP and non-MPP. A total of 763 CAP patients were hospitalized during the study period. According to the inclusion and exclusion criteria, 108 patients were eligible for enrollment. Of these, 15 patients were excluded because they refused to undergo bronchoscopy, and 3 patients declined to cooperate with blood sampling. Finally, 90 patients completed all required test according to the study protocol and had complete clinical data collected. The enrollment process is shown in Figure S1.

sec.title: Conventional pathogen detection
sec.p: The results of conventional pathogen detection, including sputum and BALF bacterial and fungal cultures, Mycoplasma quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR), a 13-item respiratory pathogen panel, and blood Mycoplasma IgM antibody testing, were recorded. M. pneumoniae antibody testing was performed using the MP Antibody Test Kit (Fuji Rebio Co., Ltd.) based on the particle agglutination method. For bacterial and fungal cultures, sputum or BALF were collected within 2 h. The specimens were inoculated on blood agar plates, chocolate AGAR plates and MacConkey agar plates (Guangzhou Diking Microbial Technology Co., LTD.) and cultured in a CO2 incubator (5%-10%CO2) (Thermo Fisher Scientific) at 35℃ for 48 to 72 h. Colony morphologies were observed, followed by biochemical identification and drug sensitivity testing. M. pneumoniae qPCR was performed on sputum and BALF samples using the Nucleic Acid Detection Kit (Guangzhou Daan Gene Co., Ltd.) with the PCR-fluorescent probe method. The detection of 13 respiratory pathogens was performed by the Medical Laboratory Center using the Sanger sequencing instrument T400, with the multiplex detection kit (fluorescent PCR-capillary electrophoresis method) developed by Ningbo Haiershi Gene Technology Co., Ltd. (China Medical Device Registration No.20183400518). The 13 pathogens included: influenza A virus (H7N9, HIN1, H3N2, H5N2), influenza A virus H1N1(2009), seasonal H3N2 virus Miu, influenza B virus (Victoria and Yamagata strain), adenovirus (B, C and E), human bocavirus, rhinovirus, parainfluenza virus (type 1, 2, 3 and 4), coronavirus (229E, OC43, NL63 and HKU1), respiratory syncytial virus (A and B), metapneumovirus, M. pneumoniae and Chlamydia pneumoniae (Chlamydia trachomatis and Chlamydia pneumoniae).

sec.title: Nucleic acid extraction and library preparation
sec.p: Three mL of blood and 5 ml of BALF were used for DNA extraction according to the manufacturer’s instructions of the DNA Miniprep Kit (ZymoBIOMICS™, Zymo, R2002). The nucleic acids were quantified and then amplified by polymerase chain reaction (PCR). The multiplex PCR-based targeted gene sequencing technology was used to identify targeted pathogens. The PCR assay utilized a combination of multiple primers and included an internal process. The PCR reaction system was performed as follows: denaturation at 95 °C (3 min); 25 cycles of denaturation at 95 °C (20 s), annealing for 20 s, and extension at 60 °C for 4.5 min; final extension at 72 °C (5 min). The product was amplified and then purified by magnetic bead method. The targeted capture and sequencing libraries were established.

sec.title: Targeted next-generation sequencing (tNGS) analysis
sec.p: A total of 476 respiratory tract infections associated pathogens (Table S1) were included in the identification model for diagnosis. The bioinformatic cut-offs used for pathogen identification is 50 reads. First, the DNA sequences were selected as the targeted fragments. The targeted gene sequences were amplified and enriched by multiplex PCR. A sequencing library was constructed by adding the sequencing connectors to the purified PCR products and using DNA purification magnetic beads for purification. Targeted gene sequencing (300 cycles) was performed on a MGI200 system using the FGS-SE60 test mode. Fastp files (0.19.7) were generated by the MiSeq Reporter software (BWA, 0.7.17-r1188) [14]. Offline data were identified and counted by an adapter and reads with a double-end length > 60 bp were retained. Among the high-quality data, reads with < 60 bp at either end, single-end primer recognition, or non-specific primer binding, were re-examined and deleted. Clean read pairs were obtained for identification and sequence alignment. Before aligning to pathogen reference sequences, clean read pairs were first aligned to human reference genome. Remained read pairs were aligned to pathogen reference sequences. Read paired counts for each pathogen were generated for further analysis [15, 16].

sec.title: Analysis of antimicrobial resistance genes (ARGs)
sec.p: All non-human source reads were searched for ARGs using The UBLAST strategies (with E-value ≤ 10 − 7) was used to search for all non-human reads against the well-curated structured ARGs database SARG, which integrated ARDB, CARD, and the latest NCBI-NR database [17]. The parameters used for ARGs identification was alignment length ≥ 75 nt, and the alignment identity rate is 80%. To further compare the distribution of ARGs in different BALF specimens, we normalized the abundance of ARGs and expressed it as the number of copies of ARGs in each cell using the formula reported in a previous study [18]. The heatmap for ARGs was built using homemade R scripts.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05, using two-tailed hypotheses tests. Comparisons between two groups were analyzed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, or Fisher’s exact test for binary variables. All statistical analyses were performed using the R version 4.1.2 software.

sec.title: Clinical characteristics of MPP patients
sec.p: A total of 90 patients (51 females and 39 males) completed all test items according to the study requirements and provided complete clinical data. Of the 31 MPP patients, 28.1% (9/31) were men, with an average age of 38 years and an age range of 14 to 66 years. The non-MPP group comprised 59 patients aged 14 to 87 years, 51.7% (30/59) were male, with a mean age of 55 years. The main clinical symptoms of adult MPP patients were cough (96.8%) and fever (83.9%), which were similar to those in the non-MPP group. However, 35.5% (11/31) of the MPP patients had yellow sputum, which was significantly less (P = 0.03) than that of the non-MPP patients (88.1%, 52/59). Headache was reported by 12.9% (4/31) of the MPP patients, which was significantly more common ( P = 0.027) than in the non-MPP patients (5.1%, 3/59) (Table 1).

caption.p: Comparison of basic clinical data between the MPP group and Non-MPP group

sec.title: Pulmonary imaging findings of MPP patients
sec.p: In terms of pulmonary imaging findings, compared with the non-MPP group, the MPP group exhibited relatively specific manifestations of bronchial wall thickening (61.3%, 19/31, P < 0.001) and alveolar nodular exudative changes (51.6%, 16/31, P < 0.001), but fewer sputum plugs in the large airways (3.23%, 1/31, P = 0.029). The most common finding was bilateral multilobed lesions, present in 48.4% of cases (15/31), with involvement of the left lower lobe and/or right lower lobe being the most frequent (77.4%, 24/31). Bronchoscopy examination revealed varying degrees of bronchial mucosal hyperemia and edema in all MPP patients (100%). Most patients (67.7%, 21/31) exhibited small amounts of white mucous secretions adhering to the bronchial walls, while 22.6% (7/31) presented with distal airway mucus plug obstruction (Figure S2).

sec.title: Etiological diagnostic performance of tNGS
sec.p: tNGS identified a total of 62 species, including 29 bacteria, 8 fungi, and 25 viruses (Table S2). More species were detected in BALF than in blood (Table S2). M. pneumoniae was detected in the BALF samples of all 31 patients with MPP, but not in their blood samples. No significant difference (P = 0.226) in species types was found in BALF samples from MPP patients compared to non-MPP patients. The top 9 species were presented in the heatmap in Fig. 1. In the MPP group, M. pneumoniae was the predominant strain detected in BALF samples, along with minor detections of Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Candida albicans and human gamma herpesvirus 4 (EBV). The dominant pathogens in blood samples were EBV and human beta herpesvirus 7 (Fig. 1A). In the non-MPP group, the dominant pathogens detected in BALF samples were Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, EBV and Candida albicans. EBV and human beta herpesvirus 5 were the predominant pathogens in the blood samples from 59 patients with non-MPP (Fig. 1B).

caption.p: Etiological analysis of the bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) and blood samples from patients with M. pneumoniae pneumonia (MPP) by tNGS. The heatmap depicts the relative abundances of top 10 species assigned to bacteria, fungi, and viruses (y-axis) and samples analyzed (x-axis) across the 31 MPP(A) and 59 non-MPP group (B). The heatmap colors represent the relative abundances of the microbial species assignments within each sample. Colors of the squares shifted towards red to indicate higher abundance. Numbers of each species in the 31 MPP samples and 59 non-MPP samples. Square colors represent the different species

sec.title: Comparison of diagnostic performance of tNGS and conventional method for M. pneumoniae
sec.p: The comparison of tNGS and conventional methods for M. pneumoniae diagnoses is illustrated in Table S3. Compared with the conventional methods, tNGS has a higher positive detection rate for pathogens (BALF tNGS: 96.7%, Blood tNGS: 50%, antibody: 40%, qPCR: 41.1%, culture: 17.8%, all with P < 0.001) (Fig. 2A). Furthermore, the positivity rate for detecting M. pneumoniae by tNGS was 34.4% (31/90) in BALF samples (Fig. 2B). In this study, tNGS demonstrated a sensitivity of 96.8%, outperforming both qPCR (80.6%) and antibody testing (67.7%). Its specificity was 100%, showing no significant difference from qPCR (100%) and antibody testing (91.5%) (Fig. 2C).

caption.p: Diagnostic performance comparison of tNGS and conventional methods. Positive and negative samples for all pathogens (A) and M. pneumoniae (B) by comparison of tNGS and conventional methods including antigen, qPCR and culture. (C) Contingency table showed the sensitivity and specificity of tNGS and conventional methods respectively. tNGS increase the sensitivity in comparison with that of qPCR and antigen (P < 0.05) while there was no difference in specificity between them (P > 0.05). MP: M. pneumoniae ; PPV: positive predictive value; NPV: negative predictive value

sec.title: Pathogens coexistence analysis based on tNGS
sec.p: According to the evaluation of potential pathogens in each patient, more than one pathogen was detected in 52 (57.8%) of the 90 patients in this study (Table S3). Among the 31 patients with MPP, 16 (51.6%) were found to have M. pneumoniae co-existing with other pathogens. It is worth noting that 22.6% (7/31) and 16.1% (5/31) of patients were diagnosed with M. pneumoniae-bacterial-viral and M. pneumoniae-bacterial co-existence, respectively (Fig. 3A). In the non-MPP group, 40 (67.8%) out of 59 patients were found to have two or more pathogens co-existing. In blood samples, the virus detection rate was significantly higher at 59.3% (35/59). In BALF samples, bacterial-viral co-existence was the most common (67.8%, 40/59), followed by bacterial-fungal-viral co-infection (42.4%, 25/59) (Fig. 3B). Compared with conventional detection methods, tNGS significantly increases the variety of pathogen detected.

caption.p: Implications of tNGS in different coinfection types. Percentage of patients with different infection types in MPP (A) and non-MPP (B)

sec.title: Performance of tNGS in antibiotic resistance and virulence factors prediction
sec.p: To evaluate the ability of tNGS in prediction of the antibiotic resistance, the ARGs were investigated. The ARGs detected in each sample and the corresponding species and type of drug class are shown in Fig. 4A. A total of 10 antibiotic resistance genes and 15 virulence factors genes were detected by tNGS (Table S4). The A2063G mutation in the 23 S rRNA gene, which confers macrolide resistance and was present in 71% (22/31) of cases, was the predominant antibiotic resistance type in MPP identified in this study. In addition to M. pneumoniae resistance genes, bacterial resistance genes were also detected. ErmB, which is associated with broad-spectrum of antibiotic resistance (including streptogramin, macrolide and lincosamide), was detected in four species (Acinetobacter baumannii, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Streptococcus pneumoniae) using the CARD and tNGS analysis results. Followed by the detection of TEM and tet genes associated with carbapenem and tetracycline resistance in Haemophilus influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Additionally, fifteen virulence factors from Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were detected by tNGS (Fig. 4B).The presentation of resistance genes and virulence factors by tNGS has certain guiding significance for clinical treatment.

caption.p: Antimicrobial resistance genes(ARGs) and virulence factors prediction. 10 antibiotic resistance gene and 15 virulence factors were detected by tNGS. (A) The Sankey diagram shows the connectivity between the ARB and their ARGs as well as the corresponding drug class based on the CARD and tNGS results. (B) The Sankey diagram shows the connectivity between the pathogens and their virulence factors based on the tNGS results. Different colors refer to different species and different drug resistance classes

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: M. pneumoniae is an important infectious pathogen causing lower respiratory tract infection [19]. In recent years, the incidence of MPP has continued to increase, particularly among adolescents and adults. This study explored the potential of tNGS for the rapid and accurate detection of M. pneumoniae in adolescents and adults with MPP. Additionally, tNGS can detect ARGs and virulence factors of M. pneumoniae, which is beneficial for guiding early and accurate clinical diagnosis and treatment.

In this study, a total of 90 patients were included, consisting of 31 with MPP and 59 non-MPP patients. It was found that the main clinical manifestations of MPP in adolescents and adults include cough, chest pain, fever, and headache. However, these clinical features are not specific for the early recognition and diagnosis of MPP. Therefore, early and precise laboratory detection of M. pneumoniae infection is essential to prevent the worsening of condition in MPP patients. Regarding the radiological manifestations of MPP, relatively specific features include bronchial wall thickening (61.3%) and alveolar nodular exudation (51.6%), which are similar to the pulmonary radiological manifestations reported in previous MPP studies [20]. Additionally, bronchopulmonary inflammation primarily involves the lower lobes of the left and right lungs, accounting for 61.3% (19/31) of the total cases. Wang et al.‘s study [21] showed that the main lesions in MPP were concentrated in the lower lobes and left upper lingual lobe of both lungs (60%), which aligns with the results of this study. This may be due to the accumulation of many secretions in the lower lobes of both lungs under the influence of gravity. Bronchoscopy revealed that 22.6% of patients had sputum plugs in the distal small airways. This may be because M. pneumoniae infection mainly affects the epithelial cells of the trachea and respiratory bronchioles, impairing ciliary function and leading to the accumulation of white blood cells, neutrophils, and other inflammatory cells in the bronchiolar lumen.

In our study, tNGS exhibits high specificity and sensitivity for the early detection of potentially unknown pathogens.in BALF samples. However, it showed a lower positive rate for blood samples using tNGS. M. pneumoniae infection is primarily located in the lungs and rarely causes bloodstream infections, so the pathogen load in the blood is too low. Infections during the early stage can also lead to negative blood tests. Currently, the diagnosis of M. pneumoniae mainly depends on serology and nucleic acid amplification. However, tNGS exhibits higher sensitivity and specificity in BALF, which may enhance the feasibility of early diagnosis of M. pneumoniae, particularly in detecting mixed infections involving bacteria and viruses. The rapid and accurate etiological results provided by tNGS enable clinicians to upgrade or adjust antibiotics as soon as possible. Moreover, tNGS can assist in timely ruling out infection in some patients presenting with clinically non-infectious fever, thereby reducing antibiotics use.

In recent years, reports of refractory M. pneumoniae pneumonia (RMPP) have gradually increased. Mixed infection is relatively common in RMPP [22]. The results of this study also indicate that the detection rate of coexistence involving viruses and bacterial infections was the highest, which is consistent with previous studies [23–25]. The high rate of viral and bacterial coexistence may be related to the fact that viral infection damages the respiratory tract, further promoting bacterial adherence, leading to dysregulation of the host immune system and facilitating bacterial growth [26]. Meanwhile, bacterial infection can increase the likelihood of viral infection by enhancing opportunities for viral replication and infection within the respiratory system. These bidirectional effects may underlie the mechanism of bacterial-viral co-infection [27]. The detection of herpesviruses in the blood of MPP patients suggests that Mycoplasma pneumoniae induces host immune responses, which may lead to reactivation of latent herpesviruses. This study primarily focuses on pathogen detection analysis, with the next step involving an investigation into its association with mixed infections and immune status in clinical settings.

Resistance of M. pneumoniae to macrolides has become increasingly evident. Single-point mutations in the 23 S rRNA gene are the primary mechanism of macrolide resistance in macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae, including positions 2063, 2064, and 2617. The A2063G mutation accounts for 99.0% of macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae infections [28]. In our study, results showed that 71% of MPP patients carrying the A2063G mutation, suggesting possible macrolide resistance. The drug-resistant phenotype of pathogens is influenced not only by the presence of resistance genes but also by the expression of these genes and other resistance factors. Therefore, ARGs should be evaluated comprehensively in conjunction with clinical features. In children, macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae can lead to prolonged fever duration, longer hospital stays, increased oxygen requirements, a higher need for intensive care unit admission, and extended course of antimicrobial treatment [29, 30]. Thus, in clinical settings where macrolide resistance rates are high in adults, fluoroquinolones and tetracyclines may be preferred treatment options.

In this study, tNGS detected 15 virulence factors in Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Studies have shown that virulence genes are associated with invasiveness and disease severity; patients in whom virulence genes were detected had higher rates of severe pneumonia (95.0% vs. 42.9%, P = 0.009), acute respiratory distress syndrome (55.0% vs. 0%, P = 0.022), and neutrophilia (82.3% vs. 62.2%, P = 0.026) compared to those without detected virulence genes [31, 32]. By targeting the enrichment of key virulence genes, tNGS can directly identify the pathogenic potential of pathogens in clinical samples, overcoming the limitations of conventional culture methods. Currently, there are few clinical studies on virulence genes detected by tNGS, and further investigation of their practical efficacy in clinical settings is warranted.

There are some limitations to our study. First, the study was a single-center, prospective cohort with a relatively small sample size, which limits the generalizability of the findings. A large-scale study is warranted to further compare the detection of pathogens by tNGS and conventional methods across different specimen types. Second, the technique cannot directly differentiate genotypic data from clinical phenotypes; therefore, antimicrobial susceptibility testing and virulence analysis require further investigation. Consequently, combining conventional pathogen detection methods with tNGS could allow for a comprehensive evaluation of infections, leading to more accurate treatment. Despite these limitations, tNGS—as an auxiliary tool for rapid etiological prediction—demonstrates superior sensitivity and specificity and could be considered a favorable method for the detection of M. pneumoniae.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The clinical manifestations and imaging features of MPP in adolescents and adults have certain characteristics. Early identification and timely diagnosis are the key to accurate diagnosis and improve prognosis. Compared with conventional detection methods of M. pneumoniae, tNGS demonstrates better diagnostic sensitivity, offers greater advantages in detecting mixed infections, and can provide information on ARGs and virulence factors. As a pathogen detection method for MPP, it has significant application value.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

Long Yuan, Zhuofan Liu and Wenmei Zeng contributed equally to this work and share first authorship.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors acknowledge Yanling Li and Lixia Lin for excellent technical assistance.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: RY: conceptualization; project administration; resources; funding acquisition; supervision. HZ: resources. ZL: writing—review and editing; WZ: data curation; formal analysis; methodology; LY: writing—review and editing, investigation; ZZ : resources. BC and DK: data curation, formal analysis, visualization, software. TW and DW: methodology, validation. TD: review and editing. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by the Health Bureau of Nanshan District, Shenzhen (Grant Nos. NS2023106 and NSZD2024044).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets presented in this study can be found in online repositories. The names of the repositories and accession number(s) can be found at NCBI, PRJNA1150745.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was conducted in accordance with the guidelines outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was approved by the ethics committees of Shenzhen Qianhai Shekou Free Trade Zone Hospital (number: 2024KY-015–01K).The participants provided written informed consent to participate in this study.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
